questions.text: I can receive criticism without it sending a jolt through my system.
After an emotionally charged event, I feel like I need to recover as if from physical exertion.
I pick up on the unspoken pecking order at a dinner party.
I find that deliberate emotional regulation during exposure to others' pain preserves both my effectiveness and my long-term wellbeing.
I find that I often need context explained that others seem to absorb from the social atmosphere.
I feel that personal freedom is the engine that drives real progress.
I tend to feel emotions at a manageable intensity rather than at their extremes.
I find that I am often unaware of the social significance of gestures and mannerisms.
I am most productive when direction has been established collectively.
I believe that short-term pleasure has inherent value that shouldn't always be sacrificed for the future.
I hold the view that someone must ultimately be accountable, and I accept that role.
I feel that a commitment made is a debt unpaid until it's fulfilled.
I believe that building wealth and security for myself is a worthy goal.
I find that working behind the scenes for others' benefit is more satisfying than being in the spotlight.
I feel that I function best when I regularly attend to my own needs first.
I hold the view that moral engagement with suffering is the clearest and most authentic way I have to express what I believe in.
I often adapt to the norms and expectations already present in a group.
I find that maintaining composure when confronted with others' pain allows me to consider all sides of a situation without bias.
